title: Chapter 7
text: Chapter Four F or a long while, Lara lay awake. The shock and fear of having a bot enter her home lingered for hours after its departure. Every sound — every creaking board, every distant coyote howl, every clang of the chimes — could be sign of the bot’s return . Exhaustion eventually claimed her . She awoke from a fitful sleep, more tired than before, to find the gray light of dawn seeping through the edges of the doorway . Lara sat up on her pallet, plucking the canteen off the floor to study it in the soft glow of the lantern. Her payment . Payment for a bot spying on her. Payment for a bot violating her home. Her arm tensed, and for a moment she struggled, longing to throw the container against the wall, to batter it into a dented, useless clump of metal . But the canteen was hers, now — a valuable item, regardless of its source — and the bot was gone. No different than the pitcher she’d given Gary and Kate. Despite her anger, she couldn’t allow herself to throw away something useful . She wanted nothing more to do with bots, but she couldn’t change the events of last night. What was done, was done . Lowering the canteen into her lap, she brushed her fingers over its worn canvas covering. All the bot had asked of her was a dance. No threats, no demands. It hadn’t even reached for its weapon. Not that it needed one … She’d been surprised to see someone at her door. Her eyes fell on his face, first, and she couldn’t ignore how attractive he was. His strong jaw, intense green eyes…but when the lantern light reflected off the metal of his hand, cold fear flooded her veins. How could she forget that night, not long ago, when she learned what bots were truly capable of . Lara shook her head, thrusting the thoughts away. She tossed the canteen onto the blankets and pulled her boots out from beneath the crate, pausing to examine them. To call them worn would be an understatement. The threads were unraveling, the leather thin and showing signs of tearing . She should have traded with Cole while she had items to spare; he knew how to piece footwear back together. After tugging them on, she knelt beside the pallet and reached under it to retrieve her knife. She stood and strapped it to her thigh. It wouldn’t help against bots, but it was something . After braiding her hair, Lara wrapped her head with a swath of cloth, leaving a portion loose to cover her face. She tucked the canteen into the fabric tied around her waist, picked up her bag, and went outside, sliding the door back into place behind her. Not that it mattered. If someone wanted to steal from her home, there was nothing stopping them. She kept her few belongings of any value with her at all times The morning sky was yellowish-gray, the air already warm. The sun blazed through the haze on the eastern horizon. It would be another hot day . Other people were stirring as she walked southward down the cracked dirt path. Some of them were covered in cloth, like Lara, ready to head out of town to scavenge the ruins. The seamstress had opened the panel on the front of her shack, already working with precise, delicate hands on someone’s clothing. Hal, the cook, had his fire pit going, thin strips of meat sizzling on the grill over the top of it . The food smelled delicious, but Lara hadn’t contributed, and didn’t have much of anything to barter with. She wouldn’t get rid of the canteen for a few mouthfuls of food. It was worth more than that . She hurried along, ignoring the twisting pain in her stomach. Already, people sat outside their shacks, quietly staring at passersby. Lara didn’t meet their gazes; she was too close to becoming like them — folk who didn’t want to deal with the bots and had chosen to give up, instead. Many of them were missing fingers or hands, result of Warlord’s justice , and their eyes were dead. Too much like those mechanical monsters for Lara’s comfort . If you didn’t want to trade with bots — whether with scrap or more personal wares — for food, seeds, and goods, you had to work even harder to survive. Why bother, when death was so likely ? “ Lara !” She cast a sidelong glance at the man approaching her and somehow kept from walking faster. She’d hoped to be gone before he rose . “What do you want, Devon?” she asked, continuing toward the water pump near the center of the settlement . “A ‘good morning’ wouldn’t hurt.” Devon fell into step beside her . “Good morning, and goodbye. I have work to do .” “You wouldn’t have to if you let me help you .” “I don’t want your help .” He caught her elbow, forcing her to stop. She glanced at his hand, eyes narrowed, and then glared at him . “It’d be better than digging through shit every day, hoping to find something to trade for some food.” He leaned closer, gaze dipping. “I have plenty to share. You’ll fill out in no time .” It wasn’t unusual for women to latch onto men who could provide. Devon grew some of his own food, owned a chicken, and had connections in the Market. He wasn’t bad looking, either. Long, wavy blond hair down to his shoulders, rich brown eyes, and strong features. Likely the cleanest man in the district. Many women had cozied up to him, and had been grateful for it . “I’d be a gentle lover, Lara,” he whispered, brushing a finger along her jaw . Lara would not be one of those women . She yanked her arm from his grasp and stepped back. “I’d rather starve .” “Oh, come on! What are you trying to prove? Why not take what I have to offer ?” “Why not be your whore, you mean ?” “Better than being a bot-banger, like your sister .” Lara flinched, gritting her teeth. Fists clenched, she turned away from him and walked to the pump . “You won’t get many chances, Lara!” Devon called as she rinsed and filled the canteen. “You’re not the only woman worth looking at here .” She screwed on the cap and bit her tongue. Rage burned in her chest, mixed with shame. They all knew about Tabitha. Why wouldn’t they assume Lara would trade her body for food, too? She didn’t allow herself to look back as she hurried south . Her heart finally slowed as she passed the last shacks on the outskirts of town and stepped onto the main road into the southside ruins. A few other scavengers were already out, moving in different directions down some of the other paths. She knew them all by name, though they kept their faces covered. Scavengers typically gave each other wide berth; conflicts weren’t worth anyone’s time . The wind whipped at her clothing while she returned to the site she’d scavenged the day before. Finding the pitcher was encouraging. There could be more to find there, with a little luck and some hard work . She paused at the spot, turning to look over the desolate landscape once again. Dark clouds dominated the southern horizon. Between the ruins and those clouds waited the endless Dust. People talked about the scrap that could be found out there, buried in the dirt; scrap that only dustwalkers went to find. But they were an odd lot. Tabitha had called them crazy, and Lara agreed . The sun was over the horizon now, casting its sickly yellow glow through the haze. There wasn’t any more time to waste. Lara crouched and dug through the rubble, working till her hands ached and her fingers were raw, stopping twice to pluck splinters from her skin. Old paper crumbled in her hands, and every gust of wind blew dirt in her face . The day wore on, uncaring for the struggles of mankind . By afternoon, her sweat and blood had only earned her a few more bits of rusted metal and some cracked plastic. Combined with her haul from the prior day, it would put food in her belly, but not much. Sighing, she pressed the cloth of her headwrap to her forehead, soaking up some of the sweat. She picked up a rectangular object to move it aside. A shard of glass fell from its bottom, clanking on a piece of concrete . Lara turned the item over. The front had a pane of cracked glass set into it, and there was something beneath. She shook the shards onto the ground and examined the photo . There were two people in it, a man a woman. He was dressed all in black, save for a bit of white showing beneath his coat and the blue cloth tied at his neck. He gazed at the woman tenderly. And she…she was stunning. Her blonde hair hung in ringlets down her back, shining like gold, and her pale skin was clean and unblemished. She wore a crisp, white gown, decorated with delicate patterns and little white beads. The woman’s smile was radiant, and her eyes sparkled . Lara wasn’t sure how long she stared at the picture; it was a timeless moment, and everything else fell away around her. The beauty and joy captured in the photo left her speechless . And it had been buried, forgotten, for countless years . A drop of water pelted the photo. Lara’s brows fell in confusion. She raised a hand to her cheek, only to find it dry. Another drop landed on the beautiful woman’s face. Lara looked up; the black clouds were overhead now, and a strong wind blew through the ruins . “Damn,” Lara rasped. She stuffed the framed photo into her bag to keep it safe. It had no value, but she couldn’t just leave it out here to be destroyed. The harshness of the world had already taken the people in the picture, but Lara could at least protect the last memory of them . She hurried her search, discovering a couple of crushed cans — a small victory. The sporadic rain suddenly became a downpour, falling sideways in the strong wind. Blinking moisture from her eyes, she slung her bag over her shoulder. She’d have to hurry back to town. After a long dry spell, the first rain was always the worst, and the roads wouldn’t be able to handle the excess water . As she carefully made her way through the debris and back toward the path, a metal sheen caught her eye. Lara paused, narrowing her eyes and turning to find the source. She spotted it in a heap of rubble. Before she lost sight of it again, she climbed onto the pile, testing her footing on the loose pieces, and reached into the gap. The tips of her fingers touched the object. She raked them over it, dragging it close enough to grab . She didn’t stop to examine it until she was on solid ground. It was a small box, fitting neatly in her palm. One side was discolored and falling apart, but the metal band around its middle was surprisingly clean. Turning it, she found the hinge along the back side and opened it . Lara released a long exhalation, and her lungs refused to fill for several seconds. When she caught her breath, she laughed, bouncing on her feet . “ Holy shit .” Nestled within in a patch of dark fabric, untouched by the sun and weather, was a ring. The band was gold, with a clear stone inlaid atop it. It was precious metal. A tiny, seemingly insignificant trinket that could nonetheless keep her well-fed for weeks . Tentatively, she ran a finger down one of its smooth sides . “It’s real. It’s really real .” The rain pelted her, stinging even through her clothes, but Lara grinned. She needed to get home. For the first time in a long while, things were going well. She’d nearly forgotten what hope felt like . She walked toward the road, swinging her bag around to her front. Her foot caught on some debris before she could stow the ring. She fell, feeling something tear in her boot as she landed in the rushing water along the side of the road . Lara’s heart stopped. She watched the box fall from her hand, watched the ring tumble out of it, watched the water current sweep the bit of gold toward a cluttered storm drain . “No!” She scrambled on hands and knees, sputtering water splashed into her face, ignoring the pain in her palms and knees as debris cut into her skin. But she wasn’t fast enough. The ring disappeared into the trash and grime gathered around the drain . “No!” she screamed again, leaping at the gutter. She dug through the mud, tossing aside jagged chunks of concrete, tufts of dead grass, and scraps of wood and cloth . “No, no, no. You can’t do this to me!” She pounded her fists into the flowing water, hitting the rough ground beneath it. It would be impossible to find the ring in the foamy, murky runoff, amidst all the rubble . “You just can’t.” Her voice broke, and she hung her head . * * * R onin watched the redhaired woman from behind a mess of rotting lumber, bricks, and sun-faded shingles that had once been a building. The raging wind and heavy rain made it impossible for his audio receptors to distinguish her words, but he detected desperation and despair in her tone. For all that, she appeared physically unharmed . Why had she come out here alone? This far from the settlement, she was exposed and vulnerable. The perfect prey for wandering reavers or other scavengers. Ronin had seen humans and bots alike end one another to seize any advantage, however slight, that would aid in survival . The woman, propped on hands and knees in the gutter, bowed her head. Her shoulders shook, and the soaked cloths wrapped around her hung into the steadily rising water. He had no reason to go to her. She didn’t want his trade, didn’t want his help, didn’t seem to want anything to do with his kind . But the emotion in her posture triggered hidden processes within him. He left his cover and jogged toward her. Before long, the road would be submerged completely. She wasn’t safe here, and neither was he . The cloth around her head was askew, and a few wild strands of hair had slipped out of it. The rubble gathered at the storm drain in front of her was acting as a small dam, creating a growing pool around her; she didn’t appear to notice . Ronin stopped behind the woman. She didn’t move save the slow, shaky rise and fall of her shoulders. Her soft sobs were barely audible over the storm . “Are you well?” he asked. It was normal for humans to cry, wasn’t it? Especially after a fall like she’d taken ? The woman stiffened, lifted her head, and looked over her shoulder. Her reddened eyes met his optics. She turned toward him with surprising speed, pushing back on hands and feet. Her skirts bunched between her thighs and floated on the murky current, revealing her pale, shapely legs . She pulled a knife from her thigh and jabbed it into the air between them . “ You !” “Have been as long as I can remember .” “Why are you following me?” With her free hand, she wiped moisture from her face as she stood. She kept the knife up, as though it were the thing holding Ronin at bay . “I’m a dustwalker. My business takes me out of town frequently .” “This isn’t the Dust .” “No. But all roads lead to it.” He almost said more, but he wanted to put her at ease. Speaking about death wouldn’t help . “Then go,” she said, wagging the knife toward the south . Ronin tilted his head, studying her. The woman’s tone was firm, voice strong, but her eyes were red, the flesh around them slightly swollen. She pressed her lips together, but it didn’t hide their trembling. Her grip on the weapon was white- knuckled . “Are you well?” he asked again, adjusting the modulation of his voice to a gentler tone . “I’ll be fine, once you’re gone.” She took a single step backward. The water streamed around her ankles like the rapids of a river. Cheyenne was a thirsty place, but it could rarely handle anything beyond a drizzle . He lifted his optics, up over one of her exposed legs. There was a scrape on her knee. A superficial wound, most likely, but organics were susceptible to many diseases and infections that could prove fatal . “You’re bleeding,” he said . “I’ll. Be . Fine .” Ronin frowned. He’d seen no small amount of blood during his existence. There was no reason for her blood to concern him, but the idea of her in pain was unsettling. “I can dress your wound .” “What the hell do you want from me?” She demanded, voice breaking into a higher pitch . What did he want? Why had he entered the human settlement with the first light of dawn and followed this woman out here, only to watch her from hiding? Why finally approach her ? There was no logic behind anything he’d done or felt regarding this woman . Moving slowly, he shrugged off his pack and brought it to his front. The woman took another cautious step back as he unfastened the flap. Reaching in with a bare-metal hand — her eyes dropped to it and widened — he withdrew the bundle he’d purchased from the food vendor . Her wary gaze darted between his face and the paper-wrapped item in his hand. Ronin extended his arm, holding it out to her. She stared, moving no closer. The rain fell loudly on the paper . “If you’re hesitating because nothing’s free, I’ll barter with you for this .” “I-I told you, I don’t trade with bots.” Her lower lip quivered . Ronin shouldered his pack to free his other hand. Taking the corner of the paper between forefinger and thumb, he peeled it back, giving her a fleeting glimpse of the spiced, dried meat inside . “I have a proposal to make,” he said, folding the paper closed. He’d purchased the food with uncharacteristic impulsiveness, but he didn’t think it would be wasted. “This food in exchange for you hearing it out .” “I…” She stared at the bundle, eyes gleaming . “Just listen to what I have to say.” He glanced up at the dark sky and wiped water away from his optics with his sleeve. “After we return to your dwelling .” “I don’t wa—” She snapped her mouth shut and dropped her gaze, brow furrowed. The hand holding the knife dropped to her side. “Fine. But I’m not promising anything beyond that .” Ronin nodded. “I only ask that you listen and give it fair consideration.” He tucked the food into a pocket inside his coat. Her eyes tracked it the entire way . “I’ll assist you through the debris.” He held his open hand to her . She glared at it, insult in her expression, and walked past him. “Don’t need your help,” she muttered . Shoulders squared, she shuffled forward, struggling through water now up to her mid-shin. When she stumbled, she waved her arms in wild circles, somehow maintaining her balance. Grumbling, she bent down and yanked off one of her boots. The sole flopped . Ronin frowned and scanned the road back to the human settlement. Rocks, chunks of concrete, and cracked asphalt were the least of her worries — the rising water hid countless hazards. Wood splinters, rusted nails, and shards of broken glass. Another open wound, especially in this dust-laden water, was too much of a risk . “It’s safer if I carry you .” “The hell you will.” She moved slowly, attention in front of her as she carefully set down her bare foot. “You’re not going to — ” Ronin closed the distance between them in two strides, dipped into a crouch, and swept a forearm behind her knees . “What the hell are you doing? I said no!” she shouted as he stood upright and held her against his chest. She twisted and thrashed to escape his hold, but her struggles only resulted in her being tangled in her wet clothing . “You made an agreement with me. You can’t listen to my proposal if you get killed on the way back .” “Even if I get an infection, it’s not going to kill me right away!” She pounded on his chest three times and then shook her hand, wincing. “I’ve done well enough on my own, so far. I can manage a mile walk home .” “Probably could. I’m just making sure of it, so I get my end of the deal .” “I haven’t even gotten mine yet,” she said, brows angled down over the bridge of her nose. She clutched her damaged boot to her chest and turned her head and shoulders as far away from him as she could . “Soon.” Ronin walked, increasing his pace steadily as he passed the deepest floodwater. Though the air had cooled significantly with the storm and wind, the woman’s body was hot against his. He shifted from a slow jog to a run . “What are you doing?” she asked . “ Hold on .”
